response:
To calculate the probability that a randomly selected student from Group B has a higher engagement level than a randomly selected student from Group A we define the difference in engagement levels as $ D = X_B - X_A $. The distribution of $ D $ will be normal with mean $ \mu_D = \mu_B - \mu_A = 75 - 70 = 5 $ and standard deviation $ \sigma_D = \sqrt{\sigma_A^2 + \sigma_B^2} = \sqrt{10^2 + 12^2} = \sqrt{100 + 144} = \sqrt{244} \approx 15.62 $.

We are interested in $ P(D > 0) $ which is the same as $ P\left(Z > \frac{0 - \mu_D}{\sigma_D}\right) = P\left(Z > \frac{-5}{15.62}\right) \approx P(Z > -0.32) $. Using the standard normal distribution table the probability that $ Z > -0.32 $ is approximately 0.6255. Therefore the probability that a randomly selected student from Group B has a higher engagement level than a randomly selected student from Group A is approximately 0.6255.

For the second part we need to determine the time $ t $ at which both groups have the same performance improvement level. The performance improvement for Group A is given by the exponential growth function $ P_A(t) = 50 e^{0.02 t} $ and for Group B by the logistic growth model $ P_B(t) = \frac{100}{1 + e^{-0.15(t - 5)}} $. We set $ P_A(t) = P_B(t) $ and solve for $ t $.

This equation requires numerical methods to solve since it involves both an exponential and a logistic function. Using a numerical solver or iterative approach we can approximate the value of $ t $ that satisfies the equation. The explanation of the process involves comparing the growth patterns of the two models and finding the intersection point where their performance levels are equal. The force of the exponential growth function is initially stronger but the logistic function will eventually level off. Nothing guarantees that the two models will intersect but the process of solving the equation reveals the time at which the performance improvement levels match.
